coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of fluorescent graphene quantum dots from graphene oxide and their application in fabrication of GQDs@AgNPs nanohybrids and sensing of H2O2
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l.
coredata.dc:creator: Mehata, Mohan Singh
Biswas, Supriya
coredata.dc:description: Graphene oxide (GO) with hydrophilic properties supports the scalable production of highly desirable material graphene. GO nanosheets were prepared with a modified Hummers method, whereas the oxidative cutting technique was used to synthesize graphene quantum dots (GQDs). The surface and structural morphology of both GO and GQDs were examined and compared. In GO, photoluminescence originated mainly due to the conductive electrons present in the sp2 localized sites and the excimer formation. In contrast, in GQDs, PL is increased due to numerous defects present on their surfaces. The pH plays a crucial role in tuning the blue and green PL of GQDs hence the optical properties. The developed GQDs with functional groups act as a solo reducing and stabilizing agent in producing the GQDs-silver nanoparticles (GQDs@AgNPs) hybrid structure, which exhibits strong surface plasmon resonance along with GQDs absorption. Further, GQDs alone support sensing of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) in an aqueous medium, thus acting as a sensing probe like the GQDs@AgNPs nanohybrids.
coredata.dcterms:subject: GO nanosheets
GQDs
GQDs@AgNPs nanohybrids
Chemical synthesis
Absorption and photoluminescence
H2O2 sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of GQDs from GO sheets
GQDs@AgNPs nanohybrids with GQDs as reducing agent
Colorimetric detection of H2O2 by GQDs (GQDs-H2O2 sensing)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the experimental set-up used to produce graphene oxide by modified Hummers method.
XRD patterns of GO sheets (a) and graphite (b).
SEM images of GO sheets with different magnifications. The scale bar of 5 μm (a) and 20 μm (b) is the guide for the eye.
TEM images of GO sheets with different magnifications. The scale bar of 1 μm (a) 500 nm (b) and 100 nm (c) is the guide for the eye.
TEM image of GQDs (a) and the size (diameter) distribution of GQDs (b).
FT-IR spectra of GO sheets and GQDs.

Absorption spectrum (left) and photoluminescence spectra (right) of GO sheets at pH~7. The PL spectra were obtained at different excitation wavelengths, 280–400 nm. The PL spectrum of 320 nm excitation was reproduced with G1 and G2 Gaussian bands.

Absorption (left) and PL (right) spectra of GQDs at pH 2(a), pH 7 (b) and pH 13(c). The PL spectra were obtained at different excitation wavelengths from 300 to 400 nm. PL spectra of 340 nm (pH 2, 13) and 320 nm (pH 7) excitations were reproduced with G1 and G2 Gaussian bands.
Schematic representation of the formation of GQDs@AgNPs nanohybrids.
TEM image (a) and HR-TEM image (b) of the GQDs@AgNPs nanohybrids.
Absorption spectra of GQDs@AgNPs nanohybrids obtained after 1 and 4 h of heating.
Absorption spectra of GQDs with different concentrations of H2O2 (a) and a linear fitted calibration curve between absorption intensity (A0-A) at 286 nm and H2O2 concentrations (b).

head.ce:title: Synthesis of fluorescent graphene quantum dots from graphene oxide and their application in fabrication of GQDs@AgNPs nanohybrids and sensing of H2O2

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laser-Spectroscopy Laboratory, Department of Applied Physics, Delhi Technological University, Bawana Road, Delhi, 110042, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Laser-Spectroscopy Laboratory, Department of Applied Physics, Delhi Technological University, Bawana Road, Delhi, 110042, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene oxide (GO) with hydrophilic properties supports the scalable production of highly desirable material graphene. GO nanosheets were prepared with a modified Hummers method, whereas the oxidative cutting technique was used to synthesize graphene quantum dots (GQDs). The surface and structural morphology of both GO and GQDs were examined and compared. In GO, photoluminescence originated mainly due to the conductive electrons present in the sp2 localized sites and the excimer formation. In contrast, in GQDs, PL is increased due to numerous defects present on their surfaces. The pH plays a crucial role in tuning the blue and green PL of GQDs hence the optical properties. The developed GQDs with functional groups act as a solo reducing and stabilizing agent in producing the GQDs-silver nanoparticles (GQDs@AgNPs) hybrid structure, which exhibits strong surface plasmon resonance along with GQDs absorption. Further, GQDs alone support sensing of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) in an aqueous medium, thus acting as a sensing probe like the GQDs@AgNPs nanohybrids.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene, the wonder material, has revolutionized the research community due to its extraordinary electronic, optical, mechanical and thermal properties [1], and extensively used for applications in electronics [2], catalysis [3], energy conversion [4], energy storage [5], sensing [6], etc. The properties of graphene and its counterparts still need exploration through their fabrication techniques [7,8]. More concerted efforts need for the scalable production of graphene for their widespread usage.

The graphene sheets already exist in nature but need to exfoliate from the precursors [9]. Among all the methods available, the exfoliation of graphene from graphite powder to produce graphene oxide (GO) by modified Hummers technique is the most favourable for commercial scalability at low cost [10]. The GO, synthesized by this technique, produces graphene-like monolayers, which have hydrophilic nature, hence dispersible in aqueous media, making it easier to transport, handle and functionalize than the native graphene [11]. Besides, GO has a unique set of characteristics, which makes it quite desirable for various applications. The functional groups, which get attached to the structure of GO, are the primary source for the multiple properties [12]. Even with the extensive study, their physical properties, which are highly dependent on the degree of oxidation or reduction [10–16] and structure, are still not well defined. The functional groups attached to their surfaces through various oxidation methods are still not clear.

The GO is considered an insulator or semiconductor with various functional groups on its surfaces [11–15]. It was reduced to quantum dots (QDs), known as graphene quantum dots (GQDs), to improve its conductivity. The zero-dimensional graphene unit, GQDs, has recently attracted much attention due to its excellent properties, like high surface-to-volume ratio, high solubility, unique photoluminescence, low cytotoxicity and varying bandgap [17]. Based on it, the number of applications in the fields of bio-imaging [18–23], sensors [24–29], photovoltaics [30–32], photocatalysis [33–35], etc., have been explored. The properties of GQDs were studied considering the blue or green luminescence when synthesized by chemical route [13,14,35].

Many methods have been devised for their fabrications, which are broadly categorized under “bottom-up” and “top-down” approaches. Generally, the top-down approach focuses on disintegrating some big sized carbon sources such as graphite [36,37], GO [18,23,29,35,38–43], CNTs [37], activated carbon [44], etc., restricting all their three dimensions to produce GQDs. Even among all these carbon sources, the synthesis of GQDs from GO is the most widely used one. This fabrication technique has the advantage of relatively lesser cost, higher yield and easier processing. Therefore, the production of GQDs from GO as a precursor has been selected and used in the present work.

In this work, GO and GQDs were synthesized using the modified Hummers and Offeman method [10,13,16] and oxidative cutting technique [13,34]. The properties of both the products have been studied extensively and compared. The unique features of GQDs make them quite desirable for the construction of GQDs-metal nanoparticles (NPs) hybrids showing enormous potentials in the fields of catalysis [45,46], antibacterial agents [47,48], chemical and optical sensing [49], hydrogen generation [50], etc. It has already been reported that the quantum confinement effects, presence of trap sites along the functional groups attached to the GQDs surfaces allow to adsorb and help in the complete reduction of Ag+ ions to silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) [51]. This property of GQDs is studied and used for the production of GQDs@AgNPs nanohybrid. Unlike the case of AgNPs, which show less stability due to ease of oxidation and aggregation properties [51]. GQDs having high stability with inherent property can also act as a stabilizing agent for AgNPs. Thus, GQDs@AgNPs nanohybrids also exhibit enhanced stability. Hydrogen peroxide is the main ingredient in some household products like hair dyes, teeth whitening toothpaste, fabric stain removers, etc. However, if swallowed in excess, it can cause oxygen bubbles in the stomach and result in vomiting, nausea and even local tissue damage. The oxygenated functional groups present on the GQDs surface act as the main factors for improving their sensing abilities [24–29]. The unique property of GQDs is also studied and used for the colorimetric detection of H2O2 dissolved in an aqueous medium.

Throughout the work, the materials utilized were standard and used without further purification. Graphite powder was purchased from CDH. Potassium permanganate (KMnO4) was bought from RANKEM. Sulphuric acid (H2SO4 concentration 98%), hydrochloric acid (HCl), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and nitric acid (HNO3) were obtained from Thomas Baker. Ethanol was purchased from Changshu Yangyuan chemical, and distilled water (resistivity, 18.2 MΩ-cm) was used throughout the process.

PerkinElmer Lambda-750 UV-VIS/NIR spectrometer was utilized for recording UV–vis absorption spectra in the wavelength range of 200–800 nm. Horiba Jobin Yvon Inc. Fluorolog-3 spectrofluorometer equipped with 450 W xenon lamp and double monochromators for both excitation and emission record the photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the samples. FEI Technai F20 ST Transmission electron microscope takes the TEM pictures operated at 200 kV. FT-IR images were taken with the help of the PerkinElmer spectrum 65 FT-IR spectrometer. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were generated with Bruker D8-DISCOVER having CuKα (λ = 0.154 nm) source of radiation at a supply of 40 kV and 20 mA.

The modified Hummers and Offeman technique was employed to synthesize GO, as shown in Fig. 1 . 1.0 g of graphite was added in 50 mL of H2SO4 and stirred for 30 min rigorously by keeping in ice-bath so that H2SO4 gets intercalated in the graphitic layers. Then KMnO4 (6.0 g) was added very gently so that the temperature does not exceed 20 °C. The reaction was then heated at a medium temperature of 30 °C for 1 h. After that, 80 mL ultra-high pure water/distilled water (DI) was added slowly under constant stirring, and a lot of effervescence was observed. The reaction was then stirred vigorously at a high temperature of 90 °C for 1 h. Finally, the reaction was stopped by adding 200 mL DI water and 20 mL H2O2 and kept untouched for 48 h. After this, the supernatant was removed and the residue was further washed and centrifuged with HCl, water and ethanol five to six times at 8000 rpm. The purified GO powder was thus obtained by vacuum heating the residue at room temperature. The mechanism of GO nanosheets formation is shown in Fig. S1(a) of supplementary information (SI) [52].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of GQDs from GO sheets
ce:section.ce:para: An oxidative cutting technique was used for the production of GQDs. In the mixture of 30 mL H2SO4 and 10 mL HNO3, 0.15 g of GO powder was added and stirred continuously for 1 h. This mixture was then ultra-sonicated for 2 h by completely covering the beaker. After this, it was heated at 100 °C for 3–4 h with constant stirring. A colour change from black to brown and then ultimately to yellow was observed, as depicted in Fig. S2 of SI. The obtained solution was then kept aside for 24 h so that all the heat gets removed and then 40 mL DI water was added. The pH of the solution was varied by adding 0.1 M NaOH or HCl. Thus, the solution contains GQDs showing green photoluminescence is demonstrated in Figures S1(b) and S3 of SI.

In the synthesis of GQDs@AgNPs nanohybrid, 10 mL of GQDs solution (pH ~7) was poured into a container. In another container, a 0.1 M AgNO3 solution was prepared and stirred for 10 min. 2 mL of the AgNO3 solution was mixed with the GQDs solution and stirred continuously for 30 min at room temperature. The temperature was then raised to 90 °C, and the solution was heated for 3–4 h with vigorous stirring for complete reduction of Ag+ ions. The colour of the solution changes from pale yellow to brown, showing GQDs@AgNPs nanohybrid formation. The solution was then cooled to room temperature and kept in a refrigerator at 4 °C for further use.

Fig. 2 shows the peak indexed XRD diffraction patterns of GO sheets, obtained following the modified Hummers-Offeman method and graphite (JCPDS card no. 41–1487). An intense diffraction peak was observed for GO, centered at 2θ value of 10.0° corresponding to 0.88 nm interlayer spacing (Fig. 2a and Table S1 of SI) [10]. Also, one small peak appears at 43°, which is due to the traces of MnO2 present in the sample. For graphite, the value of 2θ is 26.0°, as given in Fig. 2b and Table S1 of SI. The interlayer spacing is estimated by the equation
d = λ / 2 sinθ
. Here d stands for the interplanar distance, θ is the half value of the corresponding angle of diffraction and λ is the wavelength of the X-ray source. The calculated full-width half maximum (FWHM) for GO comes out to be 0.56° (Table S1 of SI). Compared to the base material graphite having d 0.33 nm, the interlayer spacing of GO sheets comes out to be more than double (Table S1 of SI). The generation of oxygenated polar functionalities over graphene layers is the main reason for the increase in GO's stack separation compared to graphite. The peak width of GO mainly depends on graphitic domain structures and their sizes, which are present inside the macroscopic graphene materials [53]. The crystallite size (D) calculated using the Scherrer equation for the as-prepared GO sheets is 14.2 nm (Table S1 of SI). The average number of graphene layers (n) per domain were estimated using the combination of both the Scherrer and Bragg’s equations, i.e.,
n =
(
D / d
)
+ 1
. The value of n for GO comes out to be 17 (Table S1 of SI). Thus, it can be stated that there are 17 layers present per domain in GO.

Fig. 3 shows the SEM images of the as-synthesized GO powder. The microstructural morphology of GO synthesized from commercial graphite powder was observed. The SEM images indicate the formation of GO sheets. The XRD analysis demonstrated that there are 17 layers per domain. These exfoliated layers get stacked together and form a porous network that resembles a sponge-like structure, as confirmed by the SEM images. The GO sheets show wrinkled texture and coarse appearance, indicating their multi-layered structure. The TEM images of GO are shown in Fig. 4 . Long GO sheets are formed, and these sheets also have stacks of thin layers that get folded together, as seen from the well-defined multi-layered structure at the edges (Fig. 4). The TEM image (Fig. 5 a) along with the size histogram (Fig. 5b) of GQDs indicates the formation of nearly spherical shapes with an average diameter of 15 nm. Since the GQDs are charged therefore get accumulated altogether. Thus, the formation of the GQDs from the GO sheets shows unique PL characteristics.

Fig. 6 shows the FT-IR spectrum of GQDs and GO sheets. The broad peaks at 3338 cm-1 and 3350 cm-1 of GO and GQDs are because of the water as a medium and indicates O–H stretching vibration. The peak obtained at 1634 cm-1 shows the presence of CC bonds, which results in π→π* transition in GO and GQDs (skeletal vibrations of aromatic rings) [12,35,54,55]. The peak at 1087 cm-1 indicates the C–O–H bond in GQDs, generally confined in the sp3 sites [55]. Thus, the FT-IR spectra of GO and GQDs suggest the bonds and functional groups present in their structures.

For the spectroscopic investigations, samples were prepared by mixing 0.02 g of GO powder in 5 mL of distilled water. The UV–vis absorption spectra of GO suspended in distilled water (pH~7) were measured over a wavelength range of 200–700 nm (Fig. 7 ). The peak observed at around 232 nm indicates π-π* transition of C–C and CC bonds in sp2 hybrid regions. There is a shoulder at 283 nm, suggesting the n-π* transition of the CO bond in sp3 hybrid regions [10,16]. Except for these two peaks, there is no other prominent band edge absorption peak found upto 700 nm. Fig. 7 also shows the PL spectra of GO with different excitation wavelengths ranging from 280 to 400 nm. The PL shows a peak at 443 nm. The intensity of the peak is optimal while excited at 320 nm. The PL spectrum is decomposed into two Gaussian bands (namely G1 and G2), considering that the PL consists of two separate transitions. The G1 and G2 bands maxima are at 439 nm and 501 nm, respectively (Fig. 7). These two peaks indicate the presence of both blue and green emissions. At shorter excitation wavelengths, the PL spectra move towards the G1 band, i.e., the G1 band has a slightly higher intensity. While at the longer excitation wavelengths from 340 to 400 nm, the G2 band dominated (Fig. 7).

It has been noticed that there is no shifting in peaks as we move towards longer excitation wavelengths. Only PL intensity is varying with the wavelength of excitations. Due to the addition of oxygenated functional groups, the structure of GO is heterogeneous. Therefore, unlike semiconductors, where the electron-hole recombination arises from the band edge transitions, the PL in GO occurs due to recombination in localized electronic states, originating from various possible configurations of the oxidation and reduction. The sp2 clusters are present in the sp3 matrixes of GO, leading to π electrons confinement in these localized states, which are considered the main reason for blue PL in GO [15,16]. The size of these sp2 clusters also contributing to the local energy gap hence to the PL. Also, the structural defects during reduction and the localized sites enhance the PL in GO. The intensity of blue and green PL bands of the GO is excitation-dependent, indicating that at least two different emitting conformers of GO are present in the excited states. Both the PL bands of the GO are quite sensitive to the pH and concentration of the solutions [14]. At lower pH (pH~2) and concentration, the blue PL dominated, whereas, at a higher concentration, the green PL dominated. On the other hand, at higher pH > 7 and concentration, the blue PL band dominated, indicating the formation of GO sheets excimers through π-π overlapping [14], which in turn depends on the wavelength of excitation (Fig. 7). The protonation and de-protonation of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups present on the nanomaterials are responsible for the variation in optical properties of GO.

The oxidative cutting of GO powder with strong acids results in the formation of GQDs, which appeared as pale yellow (Fig. S2 of SI). The absorption spectra of GQDs were measured at different pH and showed a peak at around 285, 280 and 260 nm at pH ~2 (Fig. 8 a), pH~7 (Fig. 8b) and pH~13 (Fig. 8c), respectively. These peaks correspond to the n→π* transitions of CO [30,49] or other functional groups present on their surfaces. The defects combined with functional groups trapped in sp3 sites of GQDs result in absorption peaks at pH 2, 7 and 13. It has been observed that the preparation methods of GQDs play a significant role in various optical and electronic properties. Fig. 8 also shows PL spectra of GQDs at different pH and excitation wavelengths ranging from 300 to 400 nm. The observed PL spectra of GQDs at different pH are supposed to be composed of two transitions and reproduced with two Gaussian bands (G1 and G2), which precisely indicate the position of two possible transitions, i.e., two emitting species. However, the intensity of these bands depends critically on the excitation wavelengths. In the acidic environment (pH~2), the PL bands are broad. The PL intensity increases with excitation wavelengths from 300 to 340 nm and decreases subsequently (Fig. 8a). The observed PL shows maximum intensity at 340 nm excitation. At pH 2, the G1 and G2 Gaussian bands are at 440 nm and 494 nm. The G1 band dominates at the shorter excitation wavelength, whereas the G2 band becomes dominant at the longer excitation wavelength. At pH 7, 320 nm excitation produces the maximum PL intensity of 460 nm band. The G1 and G2 bands (at pH~7) are at 433 nm and 490 nm, respectively. The PL spectrum moves towards the G1 band at the shorter excitation wavelengths. While at longer excitation wavelengths, the PL spectrum shifted towards the G2 band, i.e., the G2 band dominated. Thus, the PL band assignment is nearly independent of the excitation wavelength, whereas variation in excitation wavelength causes a change in intensity (Fig. 8b). However, at pH~13, the PL spectra are slightly broader than at pH~7 (Fig. 8c).

Here, the bands G1 and G2 are at 424 nm and 491 nm, respectively. Again the same trend follows as obtained at pH~2 and 7. At pH~13, the PL bands are well resolved with varying excitation wavelengths. It has been reported [34] that excitation-dependent emission is due to the different sizes of GQDs. However, in the recent past, some studies have also reported excitation-independent emission of GQDs [25,34]. Thus, the observed behaviour of GQDs arises due to the defect sites present on their surfaces, which might be simply due to the addition of functional groups. These defect sites act as localized surface states and are independent of the sp2 sites present on their structure. Therefore, when PL arises due to these sites, it depends on their structure rather than the sizes of GQDs. Further, as the GQD were produced from the GO sheets, the formation of the excimer cannot be ruled out, as the observed behaviour resembles that of the GO. The blue and green PL of the GQDs depends on the excitation wavelength indicating that at least two different emitting states exist in GQD, similar to that of the GO [14]. Excitation-dependent PL and PL-lifetime for carbon nanodots (CDs) have also been reported [7]. The direct absorption originates the excitation-dependent PL, not the transient trapped states produced by the photoexcitation. The multiple emitting states that give excitation-dependent PL ascribes to inhomogeneous properties of CDs [7].

The change in absorption and PL with pH is related to the protonation and de-protonation of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups in acidic and basic media. Thus, we can say that pH plays a crucial role in tuning the optical properties of GQDs. For example, when the solution was too acidic between pH 1 to 4, two bands arose: one at 440 nm and the other at 494 nm (Fig. S4(a) of SI). Also, the PL intensity keeps increasing with increasing the value of pH in the acidic medium. But at pH 5, the spectrum shows approximately only one peak at 442 nm (Fig. S4(a) of SI), and the prominence of the other peak that was present earlier nearly diminished. As the medium shifts from acidic to neutral and then ultimately to basic, the shorter wavelength PL band of GQDs dominated, which does not show any shifting (Fig. S4(b) of SI). But from neutral to basic, the intensity decreases. Thus, it has been observed that the variations in pH changes not only the PL intensity of the GQDs but also narrows down the width of the overall spectrum with increasing pH. The GQDs were synthesized by oxidative cutting of GO sheets by strong acids results in the formation of many defects and trap sites on its surface. As the pH increases, the number of trap sites on their surface increases [24,33–35,55,56]. This increase of trap sites improves the PL efficiency and intensity of the as-prepared GQDs. Not only intensity, but the FWHM also varies with varying pH (Fig. S4 of SI). Thus, the spectrum indicates that the pH could significantly modify the PL characteristics in intensity and wavelength and allow additional emitting entities in the same fashion observed in GO.

ce:section.ce:section-title: GQDs@AgNPs nanohybrids with GQDs as reducing agent
ce:section.ce:para: A straightforward technique was employed for the synthesis of the nanohybrid comprising of AgNPs and GQDs. The GQDs produced by oxidative cutting techniques have an abundant quantity of functional groups attached to their surface. When the GQDs solution is heated at a high temperature of 90 °C with AgNO3 solution, the excess functional groups get partially removed from their surface, and AgNO3 breaks down further into Ag+ and NO3 - ions [51]. GQDs in the presence of functional groups, an abundance of hydroxyl and carboxylic groups are present on the surface, thus adsorb Ag+ ions and reduce them into AgNPs without using additional reducing agents, as demonstrated in Fig. 9 . While reducing, it gets coated on the surface of AgNPs, resulting in the formation of GQDs@AgNPs nanohybrids [27,51,57]. Thus, the GQDs act as a solo reducing agent for synthesizing of GQDs@AgNPs nanohybrids, which were examined by recording absorption spectrum, XRD patterns and TEM images. The GQDs play a crucial role in protecting AgNPs surface by binding with covalent electron pairs of -COO- and NH2 groups of GQDs with d orbitals of Ag0 atoms or Ag+ ions on AgNPs surface, which stabilized the GQDs@AgNPs hybrid structure for an extended period in aqueous solutions [27].

The UV–vis absorption spectrum of GQDs@AgNPs shows a small peak at 305 nm, which is assigned to the n→π* transition of GQDs. The broad band around 400–500 nm obtained after 1 h of heating (Fig. 12 ) indicates the formation of AgNPs by the reduction of AgNO3. However, after 4 h of continuous heating, a sharp peak at 427 nm is observed. The band indicates the formation of AgNPs, which is formed by the complete reduction of Ag+ by the GQDs. The strong band at 427 nm is attributed to the characteristic surface plasmon absorption of AgNPs, which may be optimized slightly by optimizing the pH of the solution [27,57]. Thus, the absorption spectrum indicates the presence of both GQDs and AgNPs in the nanohybrid structure. A better hybrid structure/product may be obtained if both the reagents are in an equivalent ratio. However, it is tough to estimate the concentration of GQDs in the solution and the number of reaction centers on one GQD [27].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric detection of H2O2 by GQDs (GQDs-H2O2 sensing)
ce:section.ce:para: The sensing of H2O2 was examined using the GQDs at room temperature. In this process, various concentrations of H2O2 solutions (0.0–100 μM) were prepared, whose pH value is varied by adding 0.1 M NaOH solution to reach pH 9–10. These mixtures were then stirred for 30 min and then sonicated for another 30 min. The absorption spectra of GQDs samples containing different concentrations of H2O2 are shown in Fig. 13 a. A peak at 286 nm indicates the presence of GQDs in the solution. Upon increasing the H2O2 concentrations from 0.0 μM to 100 μM, the optical density (OD)/absorbance decreases from 0.38 to 0.11. It has been confirmed from the FT-IR spectrum of GQDs (Fig. 6) that many functional groups are present on the edges of the as-synthesized GQDs. Therefore, when H2O2 is added in different concentrations, it interacted with these functional groups, especially COO- and CO present on the surface of GQDs [51]. The more the concentration of H2O2, the more reduction in the optical density of GQDs is observed.

Further, a calibration curve sensing H2O2 was generated under optimum conditions using the absorbance at 286 nm as the reference signal in the absence of H2O2, with A0 and A are the absorbances of GQDs before and after the addition of H2O2, respectively (Fig. 13b). A curve was plotted with different concentrations of H2O2 being the abscissa while A0-A being the ordinate. The calibration curve shows a linear relationship in the 0.0 to 100 μM concentration range. Thus, the as-produced GQDs can be used for the detection of H2O2 dissolved in the aqueous environment. Furthermore, the GQDs@AgNPs hybrid structure exhibits a significant absorbance decreasing response toward reducing H2O2 and glucose [51] similar to that of the GQDs as an optical sensor.

To summarize, we have synthesized both GO and GQDs by chemical routes and have observed differences in their structures through various analysis techniques. The PL of GO arises due to sp2 clusters and excimer formation, while the enhanced green PL of GQDs is due to surface defects. Also, pH plays a crucial role in tuning the optical properties of GQDs. Thus, the GQDs acts as a solo reducing and stabilizing agent for the production of GQDs@AgNPs nanohybrids. The excess functional groups present on the surface of GQDs can increase their sensing abilities and help to detect H2O2 dissolved in an aqueous medium. Thus, the unique structure of GQDs can be used as a reducing, stabilizing and sensing probe.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are thankful to the Science and Engineering Research Board (SERB), Department of Science and Technology (EMR /2016/001110), Govt. of India for providing financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is/are the supplementary data to this article:
supplementary data supplementary data

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ceramint.2021.03.252.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. S. Novoselov, V. I. Fal′ko, L. Colombo, P. R. Gellert, M. G. Schwab & K. Kim, A Roadmap for Graphene, Nature, 490 (2012) 192-200.
N.O. Weiss, H. Zhou, L. Liao, Y. Liu, S. Jiang, Y. Huang & X. Duan, Graphene: An Emerging Electronic Material, Adv. Materials, 24 (2012) 5782-5825.
C. Huang, C. Li & G. Shi, Graphene Based Catalysts, Energy Environ. Sci., 5, (2012) 8848-8868.
Y. Sun, Q. Wu & G. Shi, Graphene Based New Energy Materials, Energy Environ. Sci., 4 (2011) 1113-1132.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Oh, the places you’ll go with graphene

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. K. Wassei & R.B. Kaner, Oh, the Places You’ll Go with Graphene, Acc. Chem. Res., 46 (2013) 2244-2253.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Biological and chemical sensors based on graphene materials

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Liu X. Dong, P.Chem., Biological and Chemical Sensors Based on Graphene Materials, Chem. Soc. Rev., 41 (2012) 2283-2307.
